unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit, you will explore stories that teach us important lessons about life. You will read a fable, learn new vocabulary, practise grammar, and write your own short story with a moral. Every story has something to teach us — let’s find out what!
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: A Grade 5 learner reading a fable and reflecting on its moral lesson

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A black rock burned for heat and energy
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We use coal to help make electricity for our homes.
practiceSentences: We use coal to help make electricity for our homes.
Long ago, many families burned coal to keep their houses warm in winter.
Did you know that coal is dug out of deep mines underground?
"Please don't touch that coal, Musa, because it will blacken your hands," said Mum.
At the museum, Samira saw a heavy lump of coal from an old mine.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'coal', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A small bag or pocket that can hold things
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He kept his coins in a small leather pouch.
practiceSentences: He kept his coins in a small leather pouch.
Grandfather keeps his reading glasses in a soft pouch beside his prayer mat.
Whose pouch is this lying under the classroom table near the window?
"I sewed this little pouch myself for my marbles," Aisha told her teacher proudly.
A mother kangaroo carries her tiny baby safely inside her warm pouch.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - o - u - c - h
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'pouch', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To meet or come across someone by chance
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She had an unexpected encounter with an old friend at the market.
practiceSentences: She had an unexpected encounter with an old friend at the market.
During our walk through the forest, we had a surprising encounter with a deer.
What would you do if you were to encounter a snake on the path?
"My encounter with the famous author made my whole week special," said Yusuf.
Ishmael wrote a story about a fisherman's strange encounter with a talking seagull.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - c - o - u - n - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'encounter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Areas or parts of a country with something in common
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are many different regions in our country, each with its own culture.
practiceSentences: There are many different regions in our country, each with its own culture.
Some regions of our country are dry, while others get heavy rain every year.
Which regions of the world do polar bears and penguins live in?
"Different regions grow different crops because their weather is not the same," explained the teacher.
On holiday, my family drove through three regions before we reached the coast.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - g - i - o - n - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'regions', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The morning was misty, and we could barely see the path ahead.
practiceSentences: The morning was misty, and we could barely see the path ahead.
The mountain path was so misty that Musa held his father's hand tightly.
Why does the river valley always look misty early in the morning?
"Be extra careful cycling to school on misty days," Dad reminded Samira.
Through the misty window, Aisha could just see the lights of the mosque.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - s - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'misty', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A feeling of worry or nervousness
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She felt a lot of anxiety before the big test.
practiceSentences: She felt a lot of anxiety before the big test.
Taking slow, deep breaths can help you calm your anxiety before a test.
Have you ever felt anxiety before speaking in front of the whole class?
"Talking to my mum about my anxiety always makes me feel better," said Yusuf.
Samira's anxiety melted away when she saw her friends waving at the gate.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - x - i - e - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'anxiety', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Related to the sea or ocean
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Marine animals like dolphins and whales live in the ocean.
practiceSentences: Marine animals like dolphins and whales live in the ocean.
Our class project is about marine life, so we are studying whales and coral.
Would you like to become a marine scientist and explore the ocean one day?
"Plastic rubbish is very dangerous for marine animals," the aquarium visitor warned us.
Ishmael's favourite book is full of photographs of colourful marine creatures.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - r - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'marine', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who was born in a particular place
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She is a native of the town and knows every street.
practiceSentences: She is a native of the town and knows every street.
My grandmother is a native of Mogadishu, and she tells wonderful stories about it.
Are you a native of this city, or did your family move here later?
"As a native of the village, I can show you every hidden path," said the guide.
Because Aisha is a native of the coast, she learned to swim very young.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - a - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'native', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A group of living things that are similar
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are many species of birds in the forest.
practiceSentences: There are many species of birds in the forest.
Scientists have discovered a new species of frog living deep in the rainforest.
How many species of butterfly did we spot on our nature walk today?
"Every species has its own special way of surviving," our science teacher explained.
Yusuf keeps a notebook where he draws each bird species he sees.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - p - e - c - i - e - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'species', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having a shiny, silver colour like metal
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The silvery fish darted through the clear water.
practiceSentences: The silvery fish darted through the clear water.
The moon spread a silvery light across the quiet garden last night.
Did you see the silvery scales flash when the fish jumped from the water?
"Look at grandmother's silvery hair shining in the sunlight," whispered Samira.
A silvery aeroplane crossed the clear sky while we ate our picnic.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - i - l - v - e - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'silvery', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Continuing to live, especially in hard conditions
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Finding water was essential for their survival in the desert.
practiceSentences: Finding water was essential for their survival in the desert.
Camels store fat in their humps, which helps their survival in the desert.
What skills would you need for survival on a lonely island?
"Clean water is the most important thing for survival," the scout leader told us.
Musa read an exciting book about one explorer's survival in the frozen north.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - r - v - i - v - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'survival', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Made by people, not natural
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The artificial flowers looked so real that everyone was fooled.
practiceSentences: The artificial flowers looked so real that everyone was fooled.
The football pitch at our school is covered with artificial grass instead of turf.
Can you tell whether these bright flowers are real or artificial?
"This juice is full of artificial colours, so let's choose fresh orange instead," said Mum.
Aisha's science project compared natural light with artificial light for growing beans.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - t - i - f - i - c - i - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'artificial', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Food and drink that help our bodies grow and stay healthy
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Eating fruits and vegetables is important for good nutrition.
practiceSentences: Eating fruits and vegetables is important for good nutrition.
Good nutrition gives your body the energy it needs to learn and play.
Why is breakfast such an important part of your daily nutrition?
"Dates and milk provide excellent nutrition after fasting," Grandmother reminded everyone at iftar.
In health class, Yusuf made a colourful poster about nutrition for young athletes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - u - t - r - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'nutrition', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Related to the countryside, not the city
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The rural village had many farms and open fields.
practiceSentences: The rural village had many farms and open fields.
Life in a rural village moves more slowly than life in the busy city.
Would you rather live in a rural area with farms or in a town?
"My cousins love their rural school because it sits beside a green meadow," said Ishmael.
Every summer we visit our rural relatives and help them feed the goats.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - u - r - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rural', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Related to a large land area, like a continent
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The continental weather was very different from the coastal weather.
practiceSentences: The continental weather was very different from the coastal weather.
A continental climate usually means hot summers and very cold winters.
What makes continental weather so different from the weather beside the sea?
"The map shows the huge continental landmass of Asia," the teacher explained.
Samira learned that continental areas far from the ocean often get little rain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - t - i - n - e - n - t - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'continental', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Related to a city or town
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The urban area was busy with traffic and tall buildings.
practiceSentences: The urban area was busy with traffic and tall buildings.
Tall buildings, buses, and crowded pavements are common in urban areas.
Do you think urban parks are important for families living in cities?
"Our urban neighbourhood has a library, a mosque, and three playgrounds," said Musa proudly.
Moving from a quiet farm to an urban flat was a big change for Aisha.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - r - b - a - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'urban', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A family member who lived a long time ago
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My ancestor came to this land many generations ago.
practiceSentences: My ancestor came to this land many generations ago.
An ancestor of mine sailed across the ocean more than a hundred years ago.
Which ancestor would you most like to meet if you could travel back in time?
"This silver ring belonged to an ancestor from my mother's family," Grandfather explained.
Yusuf drew a family tree showing each ancestor's name and birthplace.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - n - c - e - s - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'ancestor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A place where someone lives
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Their residence is a small house with a big garden.
practiceSentences: Their residence is a small house with a big garden.
The old stone residence at the end of our street has a beautiful garden.
Is the grand palace still the official residence of the king?
"Welcome to our new residence," said Aunt Halima, opening the front door wide.
After moving twice, Samira's family finally found a residence close to the school.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - s - i - d - e - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'residence', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To have something inside or hold something
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The box can contain many books and toys.
practiceSentences: The box can contain many books and toys.
These jars contain honey that my uncle collects from his own bees.
Does that big box contain the decorations for the Eid party?
"Careful, that box may contain Grandma's fragile teacups," warned Dad.
Musa checked the label to see whether the biscuits contain any nuts.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - t - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'contain', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something very special that is hard to explain
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It was a miracle that the lost cat found its way home.
practiceSentences: It was a miracle that the lost cat found its way home.
It felt like a miracle when rain finally fell after the long dry season.
Don't you think it is a miracle how a tiny seed becomes a tree?
"Finding my lost ring in the sand was a real miracle," laughed Grandmother.
The doctors called the baby's quick recovery a small miracle.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - r - a - c - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'miracle', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Broken pieces of stone or brick from a fallen building
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After the earthquake, there was rubble everywhere.
practiceSentences: After the earthquake, there was rubble everywhere.
After the old wall fell, workers cleared the rubble away in big trucks.
Can anything useful be built from the rubble of a broken building?
"Stay away from that rubble, children, because the bricks are sharp," called the builder.
Ishmael watched a rescue dog sniffing through the rubble to find trapped people.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - u - b - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rubble', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having good qualities like honesty and kindness
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The noble leader always put her people first.
practiceSentences: The noble leader always put her people first.
Sharing your lunch with someone who forgot theirs is a noble act.
Which character in the story showed the most noble behaviour, do you think?
"Helping your neighbours without being asked is truly noble," said the imam.
Aisha admired the noble knight in the tale because he protected the poor.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - o - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'noble', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The part someone plays in a story or in real life
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: In the school play, I have the role of the wise owl.
practiceSentences: In the school play, I have the role of the wise owl.
In our class play, Yusuf has the role of the wise old merchant.
What role do busy bees play in helping flowers grow?
"Everyone has an important role in keeping our classroom tidy," said Miss Fatima.
Samira practised her role every evening until she knew every single line.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - o - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'role', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make a deep, loud sound like thunder
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We could hear the rumble of the truck passing by.
practiceSentences: We could hear the rumble of the truck passing by.
We heard the distant rumble of thunder just before the rain began.
Can you hear that low rumble coming from the building site next door?
"My tummy will rumble loudly if we don't eat soon," joked Musa.
The rumble of the school bus told us it was time to line up.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - u - m - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'rumble', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To move slowly dragging your feet, or to mix things up
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: He began to shuffle the cards before the game.
practiceSentences: He began to shuffle the cards before the game.
Grandfather likes to shuffle slowly to his chair after the dawn prayer.
Will you shuffle the cards properly before we start the memory game?
"Don't shuffle your feet, Yusuf, lift them when you walk," said Mum.
I heard the sleepy children shuffle down the corridor towards the assembly hall.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - h - u - f - f - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'shuffle', use it in a sentence, and connect it to There’s a Lesson in That.

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

readings.title: There’s a Lesson in That: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 1! In this unit, you will explore stories that teach us important lessons about life. You will read a fable, learn new vocabulary, practise grammar, and write your own short story with a moral. Every story has something to teach us — let’s find out what!
Section 1: What You Will Learn
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and understand a fable and identify its moral lesson
Recognise features of fables and folklore, including animal characters and moral teachings
Use adjectives + prepositions correctly in sentences (e.g., proud of, anxious about)
Build your vocabulary with words about stories, community, and the world around you
Write your own short fable with a clear beginning, middle, and ending
Discuss stories and share your opinions with others using clear, respectful language
Tell the difference between literal and figurative language
Identify what makes a character behave in a certain way in a story
Fables and folktales are part of every culture in the world. In Somali culture, traditional stories called sheeko have been passed down for generations. They are told to teach children important values such as honesty, courage, and respect for elders. In this unit, you will see how storytelling connects people everywhere.
As you work through this unit, look out for the features that make fables special. Here is a checklist of things you will find in most fables:
Animal characters — the main characters are usually animals who can talk, think, and feel like humans
A short, simple story — fables are not long novels; they tell their story quickly and clearly
A problem or conflict — something goes wrong that the characters must deal with
A moral at the end — the story finishes with a clear lesson about how to behave or what matters in life
Stereotypes — each animal usually represents a particular human trait, such as a fox being clever or a tortoise being patient

readings.title: There’s a Lesson in That: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Fables are different from other stories you may have read. Unlike a novel or a fairy tale, a fable keeps things simple so the reader can focus on the lesson it teaches. The characters are not complicated — they usually have one main personality trait, which helps us understand the moral more easily.
Folklore means the traditional stories, beliefs, and customs of a group of people. Every culture has its own folklore. In East Africa, stories are often told around a fire in the evening, passed down from grandparents to grandchildren. These stories carry wisdom that has been collected over hundreds of years.
Here are some examples of folklore traditions from around the world:
Somali sheeko — traditional stories that teach values like generosity, bravery, and wisdom
Aesop's Fables — a famous collection from ancient Greece, including 'The Tortoise and the Hare' and 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf'
Anansi stories — West African tales about a clever spider who uses his wits to solve problems
Panchatantra — ancient Indian fables about animals who teach lessons about friendship, trust, and leadership
No matter where they come from, all these traditions share something in common: they use stories to teach important lessons about life. That is why this unit is called "There's a Lesson in That"!
Before we begin reading, let’s learn some important words you will meet in this unit. Study each word, its meaning, and the example sentence. Try to use these words in your own sentences as you work through the lesson.
Keep a vocabulary notebook as you work through this unit. Each time you meet a new word, write it down with its meaning and an example sentence of your own. This is one of the best ways to remember new vocabulary.
Section 3: Reading — The Camel and the Sparrow
Before you start reading the fable, think about these questions. You do not need to write your answers — just think about them quietly for a minute or two:

readings.title: The Silence After the Rumble, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The courthouse was the only building still standing in the middle of the broken town. Around it, there was nothing but rubble. Walls had fallen. Roads were cracked. Yet one room — the courtroom — still held its shape.
Ishmael lived in a small residence nearby with her grandmother. They had survived a civil war that left many people without homes. Others had moved to different regions of the country. But Ibrahim and her grandmother had stayed. “This is where your ancestor planted trees and built homes,” Grandma said. “It is our place.”
Ishmael walked to the courthouse every morning. Her role was simple — sweep the steps and make sure there was water for the judge’s bench. It was not a big job, but it made her proud.
She moved slowly and carefully, always checking the door hinges, still bent from the last explosion. Some said the building would fall soon. But to her, it was sacred.
One misty morning, she arrived early. The courtroom was silent. Then, a deep rumble shook the floor. Ibrahim froze. “Is it another bomb?” she whispered.
But it was not. A delivery truck had passed too close to the damaged road.
She exhaled slowly and climbed the steps.
That day, a native elder named Mzee Adam came to speak to students about justice.
“When I was a boy,” he said, “we followed continental rules — brought by outsiders. But now we shape our own laws.”
One boy asked, “What if laws are broken? What happens?”
Mzee Adam replied, “The law must help people — not hurt them. A nation is measured by how it treats even the smallest citizen.”
Ishmael listened. She felt a strange anxiety in her chest — the kind that grows when you are about to speak.

readings.title: The Silence After the Rumble, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
“Do I have a role?” she asked softly. “I just sweep.”
The elder looked at her with kind eyes. “Of course. You help the court open. Without you, justice begins late. Never doubt your part.”
Later that week, a trial began. A businessman was accused of stealing food during the war — food meant for children.
The courtroom was full. Ibrahim stood near the back, beside a woman in a silvery shawl who had come from an urban centre far away. She whispered, “My child got sick during the war. There was no nutrition. If that man took food, it cost lives.”
The judge entered. The people sat down. The accused shuffled to the front. He wore clean clothes and held a folded paper in his pouch.
As the trial began, witnesses spoke. Some voices shook. Others spoke clearly and slowly, trying to remember.
A boy, just eleven, came to speak. “I saw him,” the boy said. “He hid boxes behind the artificial wall in the storehouse.”
“What’s the difference,” someone whispered, “between this trial and ones from before the war?”
Ishmael knew the answer: survival. This time, people spoke. This time, the court had power.
After the trial, the accused was found guilty. The judge said, “The court will ensure the food is returned. People in rural towns need it most.”
Outside, the sun had dipped low. A pot of tea boiled over a coal stove in the courtyard. Women from different regions shared small cups, speaking of the verdict.
One woman turned to Ibrahim. “You came to every trial. Why?”
“I want to know how nations work,” she said. “How laws are made. What justice means.”
The woman smiled. “You might make a noble lawyer someday.”

readings.title: The Silence After the Rumble, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Ibrahim laughed. “Maybe. But I like being early. I like unlocking the doors. It feels like opening a promise.”
That evening, she walked home past burnt fences and empty plots where shops once stood. Her shoes crunched the gravel. From far off, she heard a soft rumble — thunder this time. The sky was grey and misty.
At home, her grandmother stirred soup over a fire. “The road may be cracked,” she said, “but we walk it anyway.”
“Yes,” Ibrahim said. “I walked it again today.”
They ate slowly. She saw her reflection in the metal spoon — dark eyes, tired but full of something new. Confidence maybe.
Later, she helped hang clean clothes behind their home. They flapped like flags in the cool air.
Suddenly, she encountered a boy her age. He had come to see her grandmother about a land document. “Do you work at the court?” he asked.
“Not really,” she said. Then she paused. “Actually, I do. I unlock the doors.”
The boy looked at her like she was important.
And maybe, she finally believed it too.

grammar.title: What Are Adjectives + Prepositions?
grammar.explanation:
An adjective is a word that describes a noun or a pronoun. It tells us about a quality, feeling, or characteristic. For example: proud, anxious, afraid, excited, interested, good, sorry, angry, kind, grateful, jealous, keen.
A preposition is a small word that shows the relationship between the adjective and the thing it refers to. Common prepositions include: of, about, in, at, for, with, to, on.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
An adjective is a word that describes a noun or a pronoun. It tells us about a quality, feeling, or characteristic. For example: proud, anxious, afraid, excited, interested, good, sorry, angry, kind, grateful, jealous, keen.
A preposition is a small word that shows the relationship between the adjective and the thing it refers to. Common prepositions include: of, about, in, at, for, with, to, on.
When an adjective needs more information to complete its meaning, it uses a preposition to connect to the noun or idea that follows. This is called an adjective + preposition combination.
For example:
I am proud of my work. (proud + of + my work)
She was anxious about the test. (anxious + about + the test)
They are interested in science. (interested + in + science)
Without the preposition, the sentence would feel incomplete. You would not say “I am proud my work” — you need the word of to connect the adjective to the noun.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are adjectives + prepositions? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: How to Use Adjectives + Prepositions
grammar.explanation:
Follow these four steps to use adjective + preposition combinations correctly:
Step 1: Identify the Adjective
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Follow these four steps to use adjective + preposition combinations correctly:
Step 1: Identify the Adjective
Find the describing word in your sentence. Ask yourself: “Which word tells me about a feeling, quality, or opinion?”
Example: Nadia felt anxious. → The adjective is anxious.
Step 2: Choose the Correct Preposition
Each adjective has one or two prepositions that go with it. Here is a reference table of the most common combinations:
Step 3: Complete the Phrase
After the preposition, add the noun, pronoun, or idea that the adjective refers to.
anxious + about + the journey → “She was anxious about the journey.”
proud + of + his ancestor → “He was proud of his ancestor.”
interested + in + marine life → “She is interested in marine life.”
Step 4: Build a Full Sentence
Make sure your sentence has a subject, a verb, and the adjective + preposition phrase.
Pattern: Subject + verb + adjective + preposition + noun
Ishmael was proud of her role at the courthouse.
The children were excited about the school trip.
My brother is afraid of spiders.
Special Cases and Exceptions
Most adjectives pair with just one preposition, but there are some special cases to be aware of.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply how to use adjectives + prepositions in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Adjectives with More Than One Preposition
grammar.explanation:
Some adjectives can use different prepositions depending on the meaning of the sentence:
angry with (a person) → “She was angry with her friend.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Some adjectives can use different prepositions depending on the meaning of the sentence:
angry with (a person) → “She was angry with her friend.”
angry about (a situation) → “She was angry about the decision.”
sorry for (feeling sympathy) → “I am sorry for your loss.”
sorry about (apologising) → “I am sorry about the mess.”
grateful for (a thing) → “I am grateful for the food.”
grateful to (a person) → “I am grateful to my teacher.”
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply adjectives with more than one preposition in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Adjectives That Do Not Need a Preposition
grammar.explanation:
When an adjective comes directly before a noun, it does not need a preposition:
“The noble elder spoke wisely.” (noble describes elder directly)
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
When an adjective comes directly before a noun, it does not need a preposition:
“The noble elder spoke wisely.” (noble describes elder directly)
“A misty morning greeted the village.” (misty describes morning directly)
Prepositions are only needed when the adjective comes after the verb in the sentence. Compare these two patterns:
Before the noun (no preposition): “a proud father”
After the verb (preposition needed): “The father was proud of his son.”
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply adjectives that do not need a preposition in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Using the Wrong Preposition
WRONG: “I am afraid from the dark.” → RIGHT: “I am afraid of the dark.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Using the Wrong Preposition
WRONG: “I am afraid from the dark.” → RIGHT: “I am afraid of the dark.”
WRONG: “She is good in maths.” → RIGHT: “She is good at maths.”
WRONG: “He was proud for his work.” → RIGHT: “He was proud of his work.”
Mistake 2: Forgetting the Preposition
WRONG: “She was proud her team.” → RIGHT: “She was proud of her team.”
WRONG: “I am interested science.” → RIGHT: “I am interested in science.”
Mistake 3: Adding an Unnecessary Preposition
WRONG: “The noble of elder spoke.” → RIGHT: “The noble elder spoke.” (No preposition when the adjective is directly before the noun.)
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Practice Exercises
grammar.explanation:
Exercise 1: Fill in the Correct Preposition
Complete each sentence with the correct preposition.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Exercise 1: Fill in the Correct Preposition
Complete each sentence with the correct preposition.
The students were excited _______ the school play.
My mother is very good _______ baking cakes.
Ishmael was proud _______ her job at the courthouse.
I am interested _______ learning about marine species.
The little girl was afraid _______ the loud rumble of thunder.
We should all be responsible _______ keeping our environment clean.
He was anxious _______ his first day at a new school.
She was angry _______ her brother for breaking her pencil.
The teacher was kind _______ every student in the class.
We are grateful _______ the food we eat every day.
Exercise 2: Match the Adjective to Its Preposition
Write the letter of the correct preposition next to each adjective.
worried ___         a) at
keen ___             b) of
jealous ___          c) for
bad ___              d) about
sorry ___            e) on
Exercise 3: Spot and Correct the Mistakes
Each sentence below has an error with the adjective + preposition. Rewrite the sentence correctly.
I am interested about dinosaurs.
She was afraid from the big dog.
We are proud for our school.
He is good in football.
The children were excited for the Eid celebration.
They are keen about swimming.
Exercise 4: Complete the Sentences
Finish each sentence using the adjective + preposition given. Add your own ideas to make the sentence complete.
I am proud of __________________________________________.
My friend is interested in __________________________________________.
Our teacher is good at __________________________________________.
I was anxious about __________________________________________.
We should be grateful for __________________________________________.
Exercise 5: Write Your Own Sentences
Write one complete sentence for each adjective + preposition combination below. Try to make your sentences about your own life, your family, or your community.
afraid of
excited about
kind to
responsible for
sorry for
angry with
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply practice exercises in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Discussion Questions
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Discuss these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor. Listen carefully to their answers and share your own ideas. There are no wrong answers — the important thing is to explain your thinking clearly.
What is your favourite story that teaches a lesson? Why do you like it?
Do you think fables are a good way to teach children about right and wrong? Why or why not?
In the fable “The Camel and the Sparrow,” Noah told Nadia she was too small to help. Why do you think some people look down on others? How should we respond when this happens to us?
Can you think of a proverb or saying from your own culture that teaches a lesson? Share it and explain what it means. For example, the Somali proverb “Aloow aqoon la’aan waa iftiin la’aan” reminds us that knowledge is like light.
If animals could really talk, which animal do you think would give the wisest advice? Why did you choose that animal?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise discussion questions and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Role Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the role-play activities below and act it out with a partner or family member. Try to use clear, expressive voices and good vocabulary. Use adjective + preposition phrases in your dialogue where you can.
Role Play 1: The Apology. Act out the scene from “The Camel and the Sparrow” where Noah tells Nadia she is too small to help. Then act out the ending where Noah apologises. Use phrases like “I am sorry for what I said” and “I was wrong about you.”
Role Play 2: The News Report. Imagine you are a news reporter. Interview Amal, the village elder, after the village is rebuilt. Ask her about what happened and how the community worked together. Use questions like “What were you most anxious about?” and “What are you most proud of?”
Role Play 3: Your Own Scene. Create your own scene with two animal characters from any fable. Act out a conversation between them that includes at least one moral lesson.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise role play and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Retell a Story
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a fable, folktale, or traditional story that you know well. It could be a Somali sheeko, an East African folktale, or any story with a moral lesson. Practise retelling it aloud by following these steps:
Think about the main events of the story. What happens first, next, and last?
Practise telling the story in your own words. Do not try to memorise it word for word — just tell it naturally, as if you are sharing it with a friend.
Use expression in your voice. Change your tone for different characters. Speak louder for exciting parts and softer for quiet, thoughtful moments.
End by clearly stating the moral of the story. Explain what the story teaches us.
If possible, tell your story to a family member or friend and ask them what they thought of it.
Section 9: Check Your Learning
Well done for completing Unit 1! Before you move on, use this checklist to see how much you have learned. Be honest with yourself — if there is something you are not sure about, go back and review that section of the lesson.
Read each statement and tick the box if you can do it confidently:
I can explain what a fable is and describe its key features.
I can read a fable and identify the moral of the story.
I understand what anthropomorphism means and can give an example.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise retell a story and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview

speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Write Your Own Fable
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Now it’s your turn to be the author! In this section, you will plan and write your own short fable. Your fable should be about 150–200 words long and must include a clear moral at the end.
What Makes a Good Fable?
Before you start writing, remember the key features of a fable:
Animal characters who talk and act like humans (this is called anthropomorphism)
A short, simple plot with a clear beginning, middle, and ending
A problem or conflict that the characters must face and try to solve
A moral (lesson) at the end that teaches something about how to behave
Dialogue between characters to make the story come alive
A setting that your reader can picture in their mind
Step 1: Plan Your Fable
Use the planning frame below to organise your ideas before you start writing. You do not need to write in full sentences here — just notes and key ideas are fine.
Title: What will your fable be called?
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write your own fable.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

Discuss these questions with a partner, a family member, or your AI tutor. Listen carefully to their answers and share your own ideas. There are no wrong answers — the important thing is to explain your thinking clearly.

quizzes.quizTitle: There’s a Lesson in That checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'coal' mean?
quizzes.options: A black rock burned for heat and energy | Areas or parts of a country with something in common | To meet or come across someone by chance | When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
quizzes.correctAnswer: A black rock burned for heat and energy
quizzes.explanation: We use coal to help make electricity for our homes.

quizzes.quizTitle: There’s a Lesson in That checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'pouch' mean?
quizzes.options: A feeling of worry or nervousness | A small bag or pocket that can hold things | Areas or parts of a country with something in common | When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
quizzes.correctAnswer: A small bag or pocket that can hold things
quizzes.explanation: He kept his coins in a small leather pouch.

quizzes.quizTitle: There’s a Lesson in That checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'encounter' mean?
quizzes.options: A feeling of worry or nervousness | Related to the sea or ocean | To meet or come across someone by chance | When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see
quizzes.correctAnswer: To meet or come across someone by chance
quizzes.explanation: She had an unexpected encounter with an old friend at the market.

quizzes.quizTitle: There’s a Lesson in That checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'regions' mean?
quizzes.options: A feeling of worry or nervousness | A person who was born in a particular place | Areas or parts of a country with something in common | Related to the sea or ocean
quizzes.correctAnswer: Areas or parts of a country with something in common
quizzes.explanation: There are many different regions in our country, each with its own culture.

quizzes.quizTitle: There’s a Lesson in That checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Silence After the Rumble, part 2': A pot of tea boiled over a _____ stove in the courtyard.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: A pot of tea boiled over a coal stove in the courtyard.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: There’s a Lesson in That checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Silence After the Rumble, part 2': He wore clean clothes and held a folded paper in his _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: He wore clean clothes and held a folded paper in his pouch.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are unsure about any word, try asking your AI tutor:
"Can you explain the word 'noble' in a simple way?"
"Can you use the word 'encounter' in three different sentences?"
"What is the opposite of 'rural'?"
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Stuck on a question? Try asking:
"Can you help me understand what 'figurative language' means?"
"What clues in the story tell us how Nadia is feeling?"
Remember: your AI tutor can guide you, but the answers should be in your own words!
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want to check your Exercise 4 sentences? Ask your AI tutor:
"Are my adjective + preposition sentences correct?"
"Can you give me more practice with adjectives and prepositions?"
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
After writing your fable, you can ask your AI tutor to help you improve it:
"Can you check my fable for grammar mistakes?"
"Does my story have a clear moral?"
"How can I make my fable more interesting?"
"Have I used adjective + preposition phrases correctly?"

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A black rock burned for heat and energy We use coal to help make electricity for our homes.
A small bag or pocket that can hold things He kept his coins in a small leather pouch.
To meet or come across someone by chance She had an unexpected encounter with an old friend at the market.
Areas or parts of a country with something in common There are many different regions in our country, each with its own culture.
When the air has tiny water drops, making it hard to see The morning was misty, and we could barely see the path ahead.
A feeling of worry or nervousness She felt a lot of anxiety before the big test.
Related to the sea or ocean Marine animals like dolphins and whales live in the ocean.
A person who was born in a particular place She is a native of the town and knows every street.
A group of living things that are similar There are many species of birds in the forest.
Having a shiny, silver colour like metal The silvery fish darted through the clear water.

selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can tell the difference between literal and figurative language.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can retell a story aloud using clear, expressive language.
